City Manager
Ronald L. Ballard

CITY OF SANTEE

Mayor
Jack Doyle
City Council
Jim Barrett
Mike Clark
Jack E Dale
Hal Ryan

MAR 1 1991

February 26, 1991

Alice Hughes
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
California Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Hughes:

As Filing Officer, my office has been working through review of
recent campaign documents filed here. I am asking for the
assistance of your office regarding one particular group, the
"Santee Chamber of Commerce Political Action Committee."

Since we were forwarding a copy of the group's latest Semi-Annual
filing (July-December, 1990) per your office requirements that
any computer-generated documents used must be accompanied by the
State's "Request for Commission Authorization" and SANPAC did not
include such*, I am including additional questions due to
my uncertainty as to proper procedures and interpretations by
your offices.

Also, I have enclosed a dossier of materials re this group from
our files and correspondence.

Background

Per page 26 of State Campaign Manual "C", pre-election statements
are required if a Committee makes contributions or independent
expenditures totaling $500. or more during the period covered by
each statement. A SANPAC representative seemed offended that I
had indicated they might have pre-election filings due during a
telephone exchange, so my memo of September 27, 1990 was sent
giving State-acquired clarification. Neither of the two pre-
election reports were filed.

* Note: The group's first semi-annual with the same circumstances was sent to your Division
        August 23, 1990

10765 Woodside Avenue • Santee, California 92071-3198 • (619) 562-6153

Alice Hughes, FPPC, February 26, 1991, Page 2

Questions

1. Review of the Semi-Annual report received January 29, 1991,
shows a payment of $850. to Hawkins & Scott coded as "P." Does
this qualify as an independent expenditure in any way that could
trigger pre-election filing? (See Exhibit B our pg. 6 of 9 -
their pg. 5 of 7)

2. Does this group - seemingly a "City Recipient Committee" become
an "independent expenditure Committee" when it spends $1000. or
more for communications which expressly advocate the election or
defeat of clearly identifiable candidates...(page 3 FPPC January
'91 Bulletin)? (See Exhibits F, G & H)

3. Page 7 of 9 of the SANPAC report (Exhibit B) shows, $1,741.46 to
Western Graphics, but no dates being required to be shown, how
would I, as Filing Officer, know whether the group made a
mailout (that is Western Graphics business) that should have
been listed in a Pre Election filing? Is it appropriate for a
Filing Officer to request additional information such as date
and sample of mailing, or is that more a State responsibility?
(Incidentally, I am a member of the Santee Chamber).

4. Were 465 Forms for Supplemental Independent Expenditure Reports
required to be filed?

5. Is it appropriate for a PAC to have an outstanding payment
($643.34) for general operations and overhead to a printing firm
(Exhibit B, pg. 8 of 9). Again, should a pre-election filing
have been done since no dates are shown as to whether this is
one bill or a collection of several?

6. Not being exactly sure due to law changes as to what may be
mailed, has SANPAC properly filed re and identified a mailing
sent to Santee Chamber residents which endorses three individuals
(Exhibit F).

7. Is a "Questionnaire" included in a Chamber of Commerce newspaper
a "mass mailing" and are there any filing responsibilities for
the Chamber or SANPAC (Exhibit P, pg. 2).

Lastly, it would be appreciated if your office would advise if I
have missed any possible concerns based on what has been filed. We
have not handled many PAC filings here and we are still learning the
changing, applicable regulations.

Sincerely,

Loretta H. Roper, CMC
City Clerk

LHR/ap
[tr/fppc.sanpac
Enclosures: Exhibits A through S

EXHIBIT B
(Page 1 of 9)

NOTE TO FILING OFFICER: If a campaign statement which contains computer-
generated information is filed with you, and there is no indication on the statement
that the computer format has been approved by the Fair Political Practices
Commission, DO NOT REJECT THE STATEMENT, but complete this form and mail it,
along with a copy of the following:

    1.  the campaign statement's cover sheet;

    2.  one page of each computer-generated schedule, and

    3.  a copy of each schedule's summary if it is also computer-generated.

PAGE 2                           SANTEE CHAMBER ECHO                    JANUARY / FEBRUARY 1991

President's Message
by Gerald Solomon

Final Report

I was surprised recently when Ex-
ecutive Director Teresa McTighe asked
me to prepare my "final" President's
Message to the membership. It was
hard to believe that almost a year has
passed already. 1990 was indeed a pro-
gressive year for the Santee Chamber
of Commerce.

At our installation Dinner last Janu-
ary, I outlined our goals for this year,
which included bringing the Chamber to
the forefront, making it an active par-
ticipant in the political process of San-
tee, and above all, serving as a quality
representative of the business com-
munity. I am pleased to say that all of
our goals have been accomplished.

The Chamber was highly visible
throughout the year, as evidenced by
our most successful year ever for our
Business Exposition at the Price Club,
Furnishings facility, the ever-popular
Golf Tournament, our Echo newspaper,
our monthly "Inside Santee Chamber"
page in the Daily Californian, our
quarterly New Member Breakfasts, and

several other programs.
Probably the highlight of 1990 was
our involvement in the political proc-
ess and the establishment of SANPAC,
the Santee Chamber of Commerce
Political Action Committee. To say
that we were successful would be an
understatement. Two of our three can-
didates took first and second place in
the Santee City Council race (Hills Ryan
and Jack Dale), while our third can-
didate (Rick De La Torre) took fourth
place, just barely missing out the third
seat. SANPAC is alive and well and all
will hear much about it during the
months and years to come.
1990 also marked the year when the
Santee Chamber aggressively addressed
issues of concern to our constituents,
such as the outside storage issue for
light industrial users and our recent
success with the amendments to the
Development Improvement Ordinance
that would have allowed the exacting
of substantial fees and offsite improve-

(Continued on page 3)

EXHIBIT E
(Page 2 of 3)

• WHAT IS A RECIPIENT COMMITTEE?

- A RECIPIENT COMMITTEE IS ANY PERSON (INCLUDING A CANDIDATE OR OFFICEHOLDER), ORGANIZATION OR
ENTITY WHICH RECEIVES CONTRIBUTIONS TOTALING $1,000 OR MORE IN A CALENDAR YEAR.

• WHEN IS A RECIPIENT COMMITTEE REQUIRED TO REGISTER AND FILE A STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION?

- A STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION, FORM 410, MUST BE FILED WITHIN 10 DAYS OF RECEIVING $1,000 IN
CONTRIBUTIONS.
- HOWEVER, A RECIPIENT COMMITTEE WHICH QUALIFIES DURING THE 16 DAYS PRIOR TO AN ELECTION IN WHICH IT
WOULD BE REQUIRED TO FILE PRE-ELECTION STATEMENTS, MUST FILE, BY TELEGRAM OR PERSONAL DELIVERY
WITHIN 24 HOURS OF QUALIFYING AS A COMMITTEE, THE INFORMATION REQUIRED TO BE REPORTED IN THE
STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION. THE INFORMATION MUST BE FILED WITH THE FILING OFFICER WITH WHOM THE
COMMITTEE IS REQUIRED TO FILE THE ORIGINALS OF ITS CAMPAIGN STATEMENTS. IN ADDITION TO THIS
REQUIREMENT, SUCH COMMITTEES MUST FILE A FORM 410 WITHIN 10 DAYS OF QUALIFYING AS EXPLAINED
ABOVE.

• WHERE IS A STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION FILED?

- THE ORIGINAL AND ONE COPY OF THE FORM 410 IS FILED WITH THE SECRETARY OF STATE'S OFFICE.
- A COPY OF THE FORM 410 OR AN AMENDED FORM 410 MUST BE FILED WITH THE LOCAL FILING OFFICER, IF ANY,
WITH WHOM THE COMMITTEE IS REQUIRED TO FILE THE ORIGINALS OF ITS CAMPAIGN STATEMENTS.

• AMENDING A STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION:

- IF ANY OF THE INFORMATION ON A STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION CHANGES, THE COMMITTEE MUST FILE AN
AMENDMENT TO THE STATEMENT WITHIN 10 DAYS OF THE CHANGE. SEE ADDITIONAL REQUIREMENTS BELOW
CONCERNING AMENDMENTS FOR CHANGES WHICH OCCUR JUST PRIOR TO AN ELECTION.

- TO AMEND A STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION, PROVIDE THE CHANGED INFORMATION ON ANOTHER FORM 410,
INCLUDE THE COMMITTEE'S NAME AND PREVIOUSLY ISSUED IDENTIFICATION NUMBER IN THE APPROPRIATE BOX
ON PAGE ONE, WITH THE WORD "AMENDMENT" TYPED OR WRITTEN AT THE TOP OF THE FORM 410. MAIL TO THE
SECRETARY OF STATE.

- CHANGES THAT OCCUR IN THE INFORMATION CONTAINED IN A STATEMENT OF ORGANIZATION JUST PRIOR TO AN
ELECTION:

- DURING THE 16 DAYS PRIOR TO AN ELECTION IN WHICH A COMMITTEE IS REQUIRED TO FILE PRE-ELECTION
STATEMENTS, THERE IS A CHANGE IN (1) THE NAME OF THE COMMITTEE, (2) THE COMMITTEE'S TREASURER OR
OTHER PRINCIPAL OFFICERS OR (3) A CANDIDATE OR COMMITTEE BY WHICH THE COMMITTEE IS CONTROLLED OR
WITH WHICH IT IS JOINTLY, AN AMENDMENT MUST BE FILED BY TELEGRAM OR PERSONAL DELIVERY WITHIN 24
HOURS TO REPORT THESE CHANGES. THE AMENDMENT MUST BE FILED WITH THE FILING OFFICER WITH WHOM
THE COMMITTEE IS REQUIRED TO FILE ITS ORIGINAL CAMPAIGN STATEMENTS. IN ADDITION TO THIS
REQUIREMENT, SUCH COMMITTEES MUST FILE AN AMENDMENT ON A FORM 410 WITHIN 10 DAYS, AS EXPLAINED
ABOVE.

• TERMINATING A RECIPIENT COMMITTEE:

- A RECIPIENT COMMITTEE MUST FILE CAMPAIGN DISCLOSURE STATEMENTS UNTIL A FORM 415, STATEMENT OF
TERMINATION, IS FILED. THERE ARE SPECIFIC REQUIREMENTS WHICH MUST BE MET BEFORE TERMINATING A
COMMITTEE.

REFER TO THE INFORMATION MANUAL ON CAMPAIGN DISCLOSURE PROVISIONS OF THE POLITICAL REFORM ACT FOR
FURTHER EXPLANATION, OR CALL THE FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION AT (916) 322-5662.

CALIFORNIAN
OCT 1 1 1990

Santee committee makes endorsements
By Laurel Faust
Californian staff writer

SANTEE — SANPAC, a Chamber of Commerce political action committee, has endorsed incumbents Rick De La Torre and Hal Ryan for City Council.

The endorsements were announced at a press conference Wednesday.

"These are the candidates best exemplifying the need for change and the ability to listen to the general concerns and issues of importance to SANPAC," said Gerald Solomon, who chairs the organization.

Founded in July, SANPAC is a group of businessmen seeking to have a greater say in the governmental decisionmaking process. It is concerned about the needs and interests of the business community, the availability of "bread winner" employment opportunities, the planned trolley extension and traffic.

"We needed to help small business owners to become successful," Dale said. "Life, period, is burdensome for the small business owner."

He listed the chores that must be undertaken — filing out tax forms and worker's compensation documents, obtaining smoke detectors and abiding by existing city regulations.

And, he added that, while necessary, this is especially difficult for small business owners, who have few employees.

"There has got to be a balance," Dale said. "We need to know in the decisionmaking process that we are keenly aware of the impact that our ordinances and policies have on the individual."

Dale said the city must continue to monitor its ordinances to determine how effective they are and there must be continuous dialogue between the city and the business community.

De La Torre added, "The city needs to foster comments from the public, not shut themselves off with a single viewpoint."

In addition to the city being attractive to new businesses, he added, it also must assist existing and viable businesses to expand.

De La Torre also remarked that the city needs more career-oriented employment opportunities in addition to service jobs.

Ryan, a 12-year Realtor in the city, agreed.

"I meet a lot of people interested in moving to Santee, and the first question they ask me is how quickly is (State Route) 52 coming in because they work in downtown San Diego," he said. "I live and work in Santee, and it's a real joy work in Santee and it's a real joy not having to drive all over."

Ryan said he would like to see more "high end" business parks like Torrey Pines and La Jolla.

Dale said there should be more doctors, dentists, insurance agents and real estate agents working in the city.

All of the candidates agreed that with more jobs in the city, traffic would be reduced.

The three candidates differed on the proposed trolley extension with De La Torre favoring the "alley route" and a transportation terminal at the old Price Club building, and Dale supporting an extension to the Town Center with a second stop at Prospect Avenue.

Dale said a stop at Town Center would ensure maximum ridership. He said he will wait until the revised environmental impact report is issued before taking a stand on which route he will support.

The city supports the Cuyamaca Street route.

Ryan also said he will wait until the environmental report is completed before committing himself to either route.(iaf)

1. Review of the Semi-Annual report received January 29, 1991, shows a payment of $850. to Hawkins & Scott coded as "P." Does this qualify as an independent expenditure in any way that could trigger pre-election filing? (See Exhibit B our pg. 6 of 9 - their pg. 5 of 7)

2. Does this group - seemingly a "City Recipient Committee" become an "Independent Expenditure Committee" when it spends $1000. or more for communications which expressly advocate the election or defeat of clearly identifiable candidates...(page 3 FPPC January '91 Bulletin)? (See Exhibits F, G & H)

3. Page 7 of 9 of the SANPAC report (Exhibit B) shows, $1,741.46 to Western Graphics, but no dates being required to be shown, how would I, as Filing Officer, know whether the group made a mailout (that is Western Graphics business) that should have been listed in a Pre Election filing? Lastly, it would be appreciated if your office would advise if I have missed any possible concerns based on what has been filed. We have not handled many PAC filings here and we are still learning the changing, applicable regulations.

President's Message
by Gerald Solomon
Final Report

I was surprised recently when Executive Director Teresa McTighe asked me to prepare my "final" President's Message to the membership. It was hard to believe that almost a year has passed already. 1990 was indeed a progressive year for the Santee Chamber of Commerce.

At our Installation Dinner last January, I outlined our goals for this year, which included bringing the Chamber to the forefront, making it an active participant in the political process of Santee, and above all, serving as a quality representative of the business community. I am pleased to say that all of our goals have been accomplished.

The Chamber was highly visible throughout the year, as evidenced by our most successful year ever for our Business Exposition at the Price Club Furnishings facility, the ever-popular Golf Tournament, our Echo newspaper, our monthly "Inside Santee Chamber" page in the Daily Californian, our quarterly New Member Breakfasts, and several other programs.

Probably the highlight of 1990 was our involvement in the political process and the establishment of SANPAC, the Santee Chamber of Commerce Political Action Committee. To say that we were successful would be an understatement. Two of our three candidates took first and second place in the Santee City Council race (Hal Ryan and Jack Dale), while our third candidate (Rick De La Torre) took fourth place, just barely missing out the third seat. SANPAC is alive and well and all will hear much about it during the months and years to come.

1990 also marked the year when the Santee Chamber aggressively addressed issues of concern to our constituents, such as the outside storage issue for light industrial users and our recent success with the amendments to the Development Improvement Ordinance that would have allowed the exacting of substantial fees and offsite improve-

Farewell to a Loyal Chamber Member

On Monday, December 24, 1990, Fares Drug Stoe closed its doors for the last time. Mr. Fares, owner, opened the store in the spring of 1960 and remained a local businessman for the next 30 years. When Fares opened, Santee was just a rural community and the only major road in the city was a narrow little league tear he considered himself pant in the community his doors to retire and spend quality time with his family. He also stated that the store was no longer conducive to running pharmacies. Although he is retiring

ments with what was commonly referred to as any "intensification of use." Thanks to the leadership of the Chamber and the receptiveness of City Staff, we were able to work effectively to limit the burdens and costs on each and every one of us.

As this year concludes, I would just like to say that it has been both a pleasure and an honor to have served as President of the Santee Chamber of Commerce. They say that you are judged by the company you keep. I could not be with any finer group of individuals than I have come to know during this past year. My best wishes to each and every one of you in 1991.

THE POLITICS OF BUSINESS

Maybe you've never really thought of it that way before. But how many times have you appeared before a planning commission, zoning board, city council, or other such body? Political decisions made every day by elected and appointed officials in your community affect every phase of your business, no matter what sector of the business community you are in.

UNTIL NOW, ALL YOU COULD DO WAS VOTE AND HOPE.

But SANPAC provides you an opportunity to join the business community. As a team we can have a powerful influence on our local government and issues of importance.

SANPAC's primary objectives are:
1. To ensure that political candidates understand the business point of view.
2. To encourage and maintain a positive rapport between the business and political communities.
3. To educate voters as to the vital role the business sector plays in the overall health of the community.

SANPAC's organizational presence is becoming increasingly important in these growth years.

THAT'S WHY YOU NEED SANPAC

The Chamber PAC was established as a separate entity by the Santee Chamber of Commerce, to fulfill the activities and programs that must be carried forward to assure you of your rights. The Chamber PAC operates autonomously in the areas of activity which the Chamber's 501C6 tax status precludes. It is empowered to solicit and receive contributions for distribution in compliance with state and local law, and issues in agreement with the philosophies of the business community. Through your financial support, the Chamber PAC continues to work for you establishing credibility as leaders within the City of Santee and San Diego County political arenas resulting in your rights being protected.

Daniel J. Viera, Treasurer

Dear Mr. Viera:

The enclosed mailing for the Santee Chamber of Commerce Political Action Committee was returned to our office recently by the Postal Service. Since you are the Treasurer of Record, perhaps you would like to see that the appropriate persons complete a change in basic information for campaign filers. Enclosed are two copies of the State 410 form used to amend information within 10 days of a change. When completed one should be sent to the Secretary of State and a copy to my office.
sections.facts:
Per page 26 of State Campaign Manual "C", pre-election statements are required if a Committee makes contributions or independent expenditures totaling $500. or more during the period covered by each statement. A SANPAC representative seemed offended that I had indicated they might have pre-election filings due during a telephone exchange, so my memo of September 27, 1990 was sent giving State-acquired clarification. Neither of the two pre-election reports were filed.

* Note: The group's first semi-annual with the same circumstances was sent to your Division August 23, 1990

Alice Hughes, FPPC, February 26, 1991, Page 2

This appears to be an official letter with handwritten responses and a receipt stamp.

President's Message
by Gerald Solomon
Final Report

I was surprised recently when Executive Director Teresa McTighe asked me to prepare my "final" President's Message to the membership. It was hard to believe that almost a year has passed already. 1990 was indeed a progressive year for the Santee Chamber of Commerce.

At our Installation Dinner last January, I outlined our goals for this year, which included bringing the Chamber to the forefront, making it an active participant in the political process of Santee, and above all, serving as a quality representative of the business community. I am pleased to say that all of our goals have been accomplished.

The Chamber was highly visible throughout the year, as evidenced by our most successful year ever for our Business Exposition at the Price Club Furnishings facility, the ever-popular Golf Tournament, our Echo newspaper, our monthly "Inside Santee Chamber" page in the Daily Californian, our quarterly New Member Breakfasts, and several other programs.

Probably the highlight of 1990 was our involvement in the political process and the establishment of SANPAC, the Santee Chamber of Commerce Political Action Committee. To say that we were successful would be an understatement. Two of our three candidates took first and second place in the Santee City Council race (Hal Ryan and Jack Dale), while our third candidate (Rick De La Torre) took fourth place, just barely missing out the third seat. SANPAC is alive and well and all will hear much about it during the months and years to come.

1990 also marked the year when the Santee Chamber aggressively addressed issues of concern to our constituents, such as the outside storage issue for light industrial users and our recent success with the amendments to the Development Improvement Ordinance that would have allowed the exacting of substantial fees and offsite improve-

(Continued on page 3)

Farewell to a Loyal Chamber Member

On Monday, December 24, 1990, Fares Drug Stoe closed its doors for the last time. Mr. Fares, owner, opened the store in the spring of 1960 and remained a local businessman for the next 30 years. When Fares opened, Santee was just a rural community and the only major road in the city was a narrow little league tear he considered himself pant in the community his doors to retire and spend quality time with his family. He also stated that the store was no longer conducive to running pharmacies. Although he is retiring

President's Message (Continued from page 2)
ments with what was commonly referred to as any "intensification of use." Thanks to the leadership of the Chamber and the receptiveness of City Staff, we were able to work effectively to limit the burdens and costs on each and every one of us.

As this year concludes, I would just like to say that it has been both a pleasure and an honor to have served as President of the Santee Chamber of Commerce. They say that you are judged by the company you keep. I could not be with any finer group of individuals than I have come to know during this past year. My best wishes to each and every one of you in 1991.

"I cannot emphasize enough the importance of the business community in the political process. Much of the legislation introduced in Sacramento has a direct effect on small business. It is not only your privilege, but your right to be a part of those decisions."

Assemblywoman Carol Bentley
77th District

"A well organized business community has a tremendous influence on the shaping of political policy. They are important to the political process because they represent the concerns of the entire community."

State Senator Lucy Killea

"A vibrant business climate is vital to improving the quality of life in Santee. We need quality employment centers to provide jobs for our residents and needed revenue to fund public improvements. SANPAC is working toward these goals."

Gerald Solomon

Santee Chamber of Commerce

Paid for by Santee Chamber of Commerce Political Action Committee (619) 562-7906

THE POLITICS OF BUSINESS

Maybe you've never really thought of it that way before. But how many times have you appeared before a planning commission, zoning board, city council, or other such body? Political decisions made every day by elected and appointed officials in your community affect every phase of your business, no matter what sector of the business community you are in.

UNTIL NOW, ALL YOU COULD DO WAS VOTE AND HOPE.

But SANPAC provides you an opportunity to join the business community. As a team we can have a powerful influence on our local government and issues of importance.

SANPAC's primary objectives are:
1. To ensure that political candidates understand the business point of view.
2. To encourage and maintain a positive rapport between the business and political communities.
3. To educate voters as to the vital role the business sector plays in the overall health of the community.

SANPAC's organizational presence is becoming increasingly important in these growth years.

THAT'S WHY YOU NEED SANPAC

The Chamber PAC was established as a separate entity by the Santee Chamber of Commerce, to fulfill the activities and programs that must be carried forward to assure you of your rights. The Chamber PAC operates autonomously in the areas of activity which the Chamber's 501C6 tax status precludes. It is empowered to solicit and receive contributions for distribution in compliance with state and local law, and issues in agreement with the philosophies of the business community. Through your financial support, the Chamber PAC continues to work for you establishing credibility as leaders within the City of Santee and San Diego County political arenas resulting in your rights being protected.

December 3, 1990

Daniel J. Viera, Treasurer  
Santee Chamber of Commerce  
Political Action Committee  
9745 Prospect Avenue Ste. 203  
Santee, CA 92071

Dear Mr. Viera:

The enclosed mailing for the Santee Chamber of Commerce Political Action Committee was returned to our office recently by the Postal Service. Since you are the Treasurer of Record, perhaps you would like to see that the appropriate persons complete a change in basic information for campaign filers. Enclosed are two copies of the State 410 form used to amend information within 10 days of a change. When completed one should be sent to the Secretary of State and a copy to my office.

Ballard CITY OF SANTEE Mayor Jack Doyle City Council Jim Barrett Mike Clark Jack E Dale Hal Ryan MAR 1 1991 February 26, 1991 Alice Hughes Technical Assistance and Analysis Division California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 Dear Ms. Hughes: As Filing Officer, my office has been working through review of recent campaign documents filed here. I am asking for the assistance of your office regarding one particular group, the "Santee Chamber of Commerce Political Action Committee." Since we were forwarding a copy of the group's latest Semi-Annual filing (July-December, 1990) per your office requirements that any computer-generated documents used must be accompanied by the State's "Request for Commission Authorization" and SANPAC did not include such*, I am including additional questions due to my uncertainty as to proper procedures and interpretations by your offices. Also, I have enclosed a dossier of materials re this group from our files and correspondence. Background Per page 26 of State Campaign Manual "C", pre-election statements are required if a Committee makes contributions or independent expenditures totaling $500. or more during the period covered by each statement. A SANPAC representative seemed offended that I had indicated they might have pre-election filings due during a telephone exchange, so my memo of September 27, 1990 was sent giving State-acquired clarification. Neither of the two pre- election reports were filed. * Note: The group's first semi-annual with the same circumstances was sent to your Division August 23, 1990 10765 Woodside Avenue • Santee, California 92071-3198 • (619) 562-6153 Alice Hughes, FPPC, February 26, 1991, Page 2 Questions 1. Review of the Semi-Annual report received January 29, 1991, shows a payment of $850. to Hawkins & Scott coded as "P." Does this qualify as an independent expenditure in any way that could trigger pre-election filing? (See Exhibit B our pg. 6 of 9 - their pg. 5 of 7) 2. Does this group - seemingly a "City Recipient Committee" become an "independent expenditure Committee" when it spends $1000. or more for communications which expressly advocate the election or defeat of clearly identifiable candidates...(page 3 FPPC January '91 Bulletin)? (See Exhibits F, G & H) 3. Page 7 of 9 of the SANPAC report (Exhibit B) shows, $1,741.46 to Western Graphics, but no dates being required to be shown, how would I, as Filing Officer, know whether the group made a mailout (that is Western Graphics business) that should have been listed in a Pre Election filing? Is it appropriate for a Filing Officer to request additional information such as date and sample of mailing, or is that more a State responsibility? (Incidentally, I am a member of the Santee Chamber). 4. Were 465 Forms for Supplemental Independent Expenditure Reports required to be filed? 5. Is it appropriate for a PAC to have an outstanding payment ($643.34) for general operations and overhead to a printing firm (Exhibit B, pg. 8 of 9). Again, should a pre-election filing have been done since
